issues.title: Independent Rendering Setting for Tabs and Spaces
issues.body: Provide the ability to independently toggle the rendering of tabs and spaces. Currently, `editor.renderWhitespace` treats all whitespace characters as a single group, which can lead to excessive visual noise in specific formatting styles. As shown in the attached screenshot, I use tabs for logical indentation and spaces for character-precise alignment (e.g., aligning function arguments across multiple lines). When `editor.renderWhitespace` is set to `all`, the alignment spaces create a dense field of dots that are uncomfortable to look at. An option to selectively render tabs and spaces would solve this. ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0f4a3e4f-996a-4591-9ea8-198369f421b6)
issues.summary: User requests the ability to independently toggle rendering of tabs versus spaces in the editor, rather than treating all whitespace as a single group.

issues.title: VS Code becomes unresponsive across multiple unrelated projects while using Copilot/agent features
issues.body:
Type: Bug VS Code becomes unresponsive across multiple unrelated projects while using Copilot/agent features. This is not fixed by deleting workspace state or starting a new chat. Logs show CodeWindow: detected unresponsive, and renderer logs show ChatListItemRenderer stuck in progressive render loops / repeated re-rendering / no-new-words / negative buffer. Files with pending AI edits (square-with-dot icon) appear to correlate with the failure because opening them triggers the inline review/diff UI. issues.title: Improve AI Chat Ergonomics: Move entry point from Command Center to Activity Bar
issues.body: **Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.** The current placement of the AI Chat button in the Command Center (top search bar) creates a significant discoverability issue due to poor signifiers. Specifically: Weak Signifiers: The icon is physically small and visually grouped with the search bar, suggesting it is a "search utility" rather than a primary coding tool. Lack of Affordance: Unlike other workbench icons, this button is non-draggable. It lacks the "moveable" affordance users expect from VS Code’s modular UI, leading to frustration when trying to customize the workspace. Broken Mapping: My friend and I spent several minutes searching for the button because the spatial mapping is unintuitive. Moving AI Chat to the top bar breaks the user's mental model of where a high-frequency tool should live. **Describe the solution you'd like** I suggest providing a first-class entry point for AI Chat in the Activity Bar (the left-hand sidebar) to leverage better natural mapping: Conceptual Model: Align AI Chat with other "core" tools like the Explorer. Fitts's Law (Visual Target): The Activity Bar provides a larger, more distinct target, reducing the "gap of execution" when a user wants to open a chat. Consistency: Reinforce the existing UI patterns where the sidebar is the "source of truth" for major workbench views. **Describe alternatives you've considered** I looked for feedback or configuration options in the Activity Bar context menu (right-click), but "Chat/Copilot" is missing. I also attempted to drag the icon to re-map its position, but the interface does not support this interaction.
issues.summary: AI Chat feature has poor discoverability in the Command Center and should be moved to the Activity Bar for better ergonomics and consistency with VS Code's UI patterns.

issues.title: Integrated terminal: VS Code injects installation root into process PATH, code CLI resolves to Code.exe instead of code.cmd
issues.body:
﻿### Problem

VS Code injects `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code` at an early position in the integrated terminal's process PATH. This causes `code` to resolve to `Code.exe` (the GUI binary) instead of `code.cmd` in the `bin\` subfolder.

Every `code` CLI invocation (`code --version`, `code --list-extensions`, etc.) opens a **new VS Code window** instead of executing in the terminal.

### Version

- VS Code: 1.115.0 (system setup)
- OS: Windows 11 (10.0.26100)
- Not running as Administrator
- Compatibility Run as admin is NOT set on Code.exe

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Install VS Code system-wide (default: `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code`)
2. System PATH correctly contains only `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\bin`
3. Open VS Code integrated terminal (PowerShell)
4. Run: `env:PATH -split ';' | Where-Object { _ -match 'VS Code' }`
   - Shows `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code` at position 2
   - Shows `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\bin` at position 33
5. Run: `Get-Command code | Select-Object Source`
   - Shows `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\Code.exe` (wrong!)
6. Run: `code --version` - opens a new VS Code window instead of printing the version

### Expected Behavior

`code` should resolve to `code.cmd` in `bin\`, which sets ELECTRON_RUN_AS_NODE=1 and runs cli.js correctly.

### Root Cause

VS Code prepends its installation directory (containing Code.exe) to the terminal's process PATH, shadowing the correct `bin\code.cmd`.

### Impact on Copilot Agent Mode

GitHub Copilot agent mode uses run_in_terminal to execute `code --list-extensions` and similar CLI commands. Each invocation spawns a new VS Code window without the user's consent. Especially disruptive during automated multi-step tasks.

### Workaround

In settings.json:
`terminal.integrated.env.windows`: { `PATH`: `C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\bin;env:PATH` }

This prepends bin\ so code.cmd takes priority over the injected Code.exe.

### Note

This is NOT the admin/elevated terminal issue (#91613, fixed in 1.110). This occurs in a normal non-elevated terminal session.
issues.summary: VS Code injects its installation root into terminal PATH, causing the `code` CLI command to resolve to Code.exe instead of code.cmd, opening new windows instead of executing CLI commands.

issues.title: Automatically update in Administrator in Windows
issues.body:
<!-- ⚠️⚠️ Do Not Delete This! feature_request_template ⚠️⚠️ -->
<!-- Please read our Rules of Conduct: https://opensource.microsoft.com/codeofconduct/ -->
<!-- Please search existing issues to avoid creating duplicates. -->

<!-- Describe the feature you'd like. -->

1. I always working on Administrator.
2. I want user-scope installation, I don't want system wide installation because I don't need to install vscode for other users.
3. I want VSCode automatically update.

Now it pops up 
```
Updates are disabled because you are running the user-scope installation of Visual Studio Code as Administrator.
```
Every time it stats up. And auto-update doesn't work, I have to download the user-scope installer and manually install it on each new version. 

Please add a option or something can bypass this restriction.
issues.summary: User-scope VSCode installation running as Administrator prevents automatic updates, requiring manual reinstallation for each version.

issues.title: read_file / replace_string_in_file blocked by ifNoneMatch content exclusion on existing files
issues.body:
## Description

`read_file` and `replace_string_in_file` tools are blocked by content exclusion `ifNoneMatch` rules, even on **existing** text files. This is a broader variant of #297198, which fixed the `create_file` case (file-not-found → treated as excluded). The same `ifNoneMatch` content-sniffing logic also incorrectly excludes existing files in some cases.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Be in an org that has server-side `ifNoneMatch` content exclusion rules.
2. Open any workspace — even files in `/tmp` (outside any git repo) are affected.
3. Ask Copilot agent to read or edit a plain text file (`.md`, `.py`, `.c`, etc.) using `read_file` or `replace_string_in_file`.
4. The tool fails with a content exclusion error.

## Expected behavior

`read_file` and `replace_string_in_file` should work on text files that are not matched by any exclusion patte`read_file` and `replace_string_in_file` oc`read_file` and `replace_string_in_file` should work on text files that are not matched by any exclusion
## ## ## ## ## ## ## ## ##l c## ## ## ## ## ## ## ## ##l c## ## ## ## ## ## ## ## ##l c## ## ## ## ## ## ## ## ##l c## ## ## ## ## ## ## ## ##l c## ## ## ## ## ## ## ## ##l c## ## ## ## ## ## ## ## ##l c## ## ## ## ## ## ## ## ##l c## ## ## ## ## ## ## ## ##l c## ## ## ## ## ## ## ## ##l c## ## ## ## ## ## ## ## ##l c## ## ## ## ## ## ## ## ##l c## ## ## ## ## ## ## ## ##l c#981## ## ## #licate of #297198
issues.summary: read_file and replace_string_in_file tools are incorrectly blocked by content exclusion rules on existing text files that should be accessible.

issues.title: Fix 5 chat widget disposal leaks and add comprehensive leak detection tests
issues.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes 5 disposal leaks in the chat widget rendering pipeline and adds a comprehensive test suite to detect them. The test suite uses a three-snapshot strategy (before load, before unload, after unload) to verify that every disposable created during rendering is properly cleaned up when the model is removed.

## Production Fixes

### 1. `chatListRenderer. Clear content parts in `disposeElement`ts` 
When list items leave the viewport, content parts (markdown, code blocks, tool invocations, etc.) were not being disposed. Added `clearRenderedParts(templateData)` in `disposeElement` to prevent accumulation in the row cache.

### 2. `chatWidget. Clear list on model unloadts` 
When `setModel(undefined)` is called, the list widget was not being notified, leaving tree items alive. Added `this.listWidget.setViewModel(undefined)` to trigger proper item removal and disposal.

### 3. `chatInputPart. Register input text modelts` 
The input editor's text model was created but never registered for disposal, causing it to leak when the widget was disposed. Added `this._register(inputModel)`.

### 4. `chatTreeContentPart. Track tree in DisposableStorets` 
The `WorkbenchCompressibleAsyncDataTree` created in the tree pool was not added to the pool's `DisposableStore`, so it was never disposed on release. Wrapped creation in `store.add()`.

### 5. `chatModel. Null guard in progressTask callbackts` 
The `.then()` callback on progress tasks could crash if response parts had been cleared before the task settled. Added a null guard.

## Test Suite (2 suites, 9 tests)

**Suite  uses `ensureNoDisposablesAreLeakedInTestSuite`:1** 
- Load completed model with 22+ content types (markdown, code blocks, edits, tool calls, confirmations, thinking, progress, trees, etc.)
- Progressive streaming with accepted/rejected tool confirmations
- Model switching between sessions
- Widget lifecycle without model

**Suite  uses manual `DisposableTracker` with load-phase tracking:2** 
- Load/unload cycle: verifies rendering disposables are cleaned up
- Model switching: verifies no artifacts survive from previous model
- 5 repeated cycles: verifies no cumulative growth

### Content types covered
Markdown, fenced code blocks, edit code blocks (collapsed pill), tool invocations (running/complete/failed), tool confirmations (accepted/rejected), thinking (collapsible/non-collapsible), progress messages, workspace edits, command buttons, content references, file trees, external tool invocations, warning messages, and more.

### Mutation testing
All 5 fixes were individually reverted and confirmed caught by the test suite. Additional probing with 14 realistic mutations across content part files verified broad regression detection.
issues.summary: Multiple memory leaks in chat widget disposal pipeline are fixed with comprehensive leak detection tests to prevent regression.
